title: Common Contract Definitions
description: Shared v1 definitions (BBL, BIN, borough, timestamps) referenced by the other canonical contracts. Every value-shape here is grounded in official outputs documented in docs/research/M0-T002-geoclient-address-resolution.md (retrieved 2026-07-14); nothing is invented. Where official documentation leaves a constraint open, the description says so explicitly (open-with-flag) instead of guessing.

bbl.description: 10-digit NYC Borough-Block-Lot: 1 borough digit (1-5) + 5-digit zero-padded tax block + 4-digit zero-padded tax lot. Grounding: Geoclient v2 /bbl example normalizes block '67' -> bblTaxBlock '00067', lot '1' -> bblTaxLot '0001', bbl '1000670001'; /v2/search recognition rule: 'A ten-digit number where the first digit is 1-5 is recognized as a BBL request' (Geoclient User Guide v2.0.4, https://mlipper.github.io/geoclient/; docs/research/M0-T002-geoclient-address-resolution.md sections 2.6-2.7). The pattern rejects borough 0 and 6-9, wrong length, and non-numeric input. OPEN-WITH-FLAG: official documentation does not state whether all-zero block (00000) or all-zero lot (0000) can exist, so the pattern does not exclude them; lot EXISTENCE validation is delegated to the Geoclient Function BL connector, never to this pattern.

bin.description: 7-digit NYC Building Identification Number, first digit = borough (1-5). Grounding: Geoclient /v2/search recognition rule: 'A seven-digit number where the first digit is 1-5 is recognized as a BIN request'; documented example BINs '1057127', '1079043' (Geoclient User Guide v2.0.4; docs/research/M0-T002-geoclient-address-resolution.md sections 2.6-2.7) and live GeoSearch addendum.pad.bin '1001026' (same doc, section 3).

borough_code.description: Official NYC borough number: Manhattan=1, Bronx=2, Brooklyn=3, Queens=4, Staten Island=5. Grounding: Geoclient User Guide v2.0.4 section 2.2.1 borough values 'Manhattan/MN/1, Bronx/BX/2, Brooklyn/BK/BKLYN/3, Queens/QN/4, Staten Island/SI/5' (docs/research/M0-T002-geoclient-address-resolution.md section 2.2). This is the canonical machine representation; it equals the first digit of the BBL and BIN.

borough_name.description: Normalized borough display name using the spellings documented in Geoclient User Guide v2.0.4 section 2.2.1 and observed in the live GeoSearch v2 response ('borough': 'Manhattan') captured in docs/research/M0-T002-geoclient-address-resolution.md section 3 (retrieved 2026-07-14). Raw connector spellings (including case variants such as uppercase Geosupport names) are preserved verbatim in the provenance record's original_value; normalization to this enum is case-insensitive, consistent with Geoclient's documented case-insensitivity of parameter values (User Guide section 2.2).

zip_code.description: 5-digit USPS ZIP code as observed in official outputs (Geoclient 'zipCode', GeoSearch 'postalcode' e.g. '10271'; docs/research/M0-T002-geoclient-address-resolution.md sections 2.6 and 3). OPEN-WITH-FLAG: ZIP+4 has not been observed in the documented official outputs; if a connector returns it, relax this pattern additively rather than truncating data.

date_time.description: RFC 3339 timestamp. The pattern is enforced (not only the 'format' annotation) so validation behaves identically whether or not a format-aware validator is installed.

date.description: RFC 3339 full-date. Pattern enforced for validator-independent behavior.

non_empty_string.description: String that must not be empty.

digest_sha256.description: Canonical content digest (task M2-T004, additive): 'sha256:' + lowercase-hex SHA-256 of the UTF-8 encoding of the canonical JSON serialization of the PARSED value being digested. Canonical JSON form (spec id 'canonical-json-1', recorded verbatim per profile in reproducibility.digest_canonicalization): object keys sorted lexicographically by Unicode code point; separators ',' and ':' with no insignificant whitespace; non-ASCII characters preserved as-is (not escaped); no Unicode normalization applied; numbers serialized by Python json.dumps defaults (integers verbatim, floats shortest-repr). Digesting the parsed value rather than raw bytes makes the digest independent of source key order and insignificant whitespace, so byte-different but semantically identical responses digest equal, while any value change flips the digest.
